outbound_official.notes: The Polish Ministry of Foreign Affairs (MSZ) publishes per-country travel profiles at https://www.gov.pl/web/dyplomacja/informacje-dla-podrozujacych covering approximately 200 destinations. Each country profile includes a 'Wjazd i pobyt' (Entry and Stay) section stating whether Polish ordinary-passport holders need a visa and what documents are required. This is the authoritative origin-side resource for Polish outbound travel. There is NO single consolidated outbound visa-free list (analogous to India's mea.gov.in/vffin); entry requirements must be looked up per destination country. The per-country profiles are authored by Polish diplomatic posts abroad. The visa-free, visa_on_arrival, e_visa, and eta arrays are left empty here because the MFA publishes this data only at the individual country-profile level, not as an aggregated list.

Biometric (ICAO-compliant) passport required; 90 days within any 180-day period. Kosovo was transferred from Annex I to Annex II of Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 by Regulation (EU) 2023/850 of 19 April 2023; the visa exemption applies from 1 January 2024. Source: EC Home Affairs announcement 2024-01-03 and EUR-Lex consolidated text of Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 as of 03.02.2025.
All EU and EEA citizens enjoy freedom of movement; no visa or stay limit applies under EU law

visa_policy.notes: Poland is a Schengen member. Visa-free visitors from third countries are generally permitted 90 days within any 180-day period under the standard Schengen rule. The visa exemption does not cover residence, work, or paid activity. Several Western Balkan and Eastern Partnership countries require biometric passports. Nationals of Afghanistan, Armenia (from 1 August 2022), Bangladesh, Democratic Republic of Congo, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Iran, Iraq, Nigeria, Pakistan, Somalia, and Sri Lanka require an airport transit visa (A-type) even when transiting through Polish airports (per gov.pl/usa-en/a-type-airport-transit-visa - 13 nationalities; Cuba and Egypt are NOT on the official Polish gov.pl A-type list). Poland does not operate a separate e-visa or ETA system; standard Schengen C-type visas are applied for via the e-Konsulat portal (secure.e-konsulat.gov.pl) at Polish embassies/consulates. VANUATU REMOVED: Vanuatu was formally removed from the EU Schengen Annex II visa-free list by EU Regulation 2025/11 adopted 19 December 2024, entering into force 3 February 2025 (preceded by temporary suspension under delegated regulation from 4 August 2024). Vanuatu nationals now require a Schengen visa. Some official Polish gov.pl embassy pages have not yet been updated and still list Vanuatu - the governing EU regulation takes precedence. Saint Pierre and Miquelon is a French overseas collectivity; French EU citizen freedom-of-movement applies; the entry on official lists likely refers to travel document holders who are French nationals. KOSOVO ADDED: Kosovo nationals are visa-free for Schengen (including Poland) from 1 January 2024, per Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806 (Kosovo listed with footnote that the exemption applies from the date ETIAS starts operations or 1 January 2024, whichever is earlier; since 1 January 2024 has passed, the exemption is in force). ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) is expected to launch in mid-to-late 2026 and will require pre-travel authorisation for visa-exempt non-EU nationals; it was not yet operational as of June 2026.

credential.subtype: any valid residence permit issued by another Schengen Member State

conditional_access.conditions: Foreign nationals holding a valid residence permit issued by another Schengen Member State are entitled to stay in Schengen Member States other than the one which issued the permit (including Poland) during the validity of that residence permit for a period not exceeding 90 days in any 180-day period. Traveller must carry the residence permit and a valid travel document.

credential.subtype: Type D (national/long-stay visa) issued by another Schengen Member State

conditional_access.conditions: Foreign nationals holding a valid long-stay (D-type) visa issued by another Schengen Member State are entitled to stay in Schengen Member States other than the one which issued the visa (including Poland) during the validity of that visa for a period not exceeding 90 days in any 180-day period. Traveller must carry the D-type visa and a valid travel document.

cbi.notes: Poland has no citizenship-by-investment program. The official Ministry of the Interior and Administration page on citizenship recognition does not mention any investment route. Polish citizenship can be granted by the President in exceptional cases (e.g. for special merits), but this is a discretionary presidential prerogative, not a formal investment program. Source: https://www.gov.pl/web/mswia-en/apply-to-be-recognised-as-a-polish-citizen

programs.name: Temporary Residence Permit for Conducting Business Activity
programs.notes: Available to non-EU nationals operating a sole proprietorship in the CEIDG register, holding shares/management board membership in a Polish limited liability or joint-stock company, or acting as a general partner in a limited partnership. The business must generate income not lower than twelve times the average monthly remuneration for the prior tax year. Minimum personal income requirement: PLN 1,010 net/month for a single applicant (2026 figures). No statutory minimum investment amount; program requires genuine business activity. Permit issued for up to 3 years. Permanent residence after 5 years of uninterrupted temporary residence; citizenship eligibility after further period. Applications submitted via the MOS portal (mos.cudzoziemcy.gov.pl) from April 2026.

programs.name: Temporary Residence Permit for Conducting Business Activity (Company Director / Partner)
programs.notes: Specifically for management board members of a sp. z o.o. that the applicant appointed or of which they are a partner, or general/limited partners in partnerships. Same income conditions as general business activity permit. Issued for up to 3 years.

programs.name: EU Blue Card (Karta Niebieska UE)
programs.eligibility: Non-EU nationals with a valid work contract of at least 1 year for highly qualified employment (higher education degree or 5 years relevant professional experience); gross monthly salary of at least PLN 9,519.23
programs.processing_time: Maximum 60 days from submission of complete application
programs.notes: The EU Blue Card is a single permit for residence and work; no separate work permit needed. Permit issued for the duration of the employment contract plus 3 months, up to a maximum of 3 years. Application fee: PLN 440. Minimum gross monthly salary threshold: PLN 9,519.23 (as confirmed on the European Commission EU immigration portal). Labour market test applies in Poland. Source also: https://home-affairs.ec.europa.eu/policies/migration-and-asylum/eu-immigration-portal/eu-blue-card/eu-blue-card-poland_en

programs.name: Poland Business Harbour (PBH)
programs.eligibility: Originally for IT/ICT specialists and entrepreneurs (initially from Belarus, later expanded to Georgia, Russia, Ukraine, Moldova, Armenia); required a partnership with a Polish company or founding own business in Poland. D23 visa type granted without separate work permit.
programs.processing_time: Approximately 2-5 weeks for visa; ~30 days for company verification
programs.notes: Program is SUSPENDED – Minister of Foreign Affairs suspended MFA participation on 26 January 2024 due to concerns about inadequate verification of beneficiary companies and misuse of visas. No new visas or recommendations are being issued. Visas already issued remain valid. The MFA intends to develop streamlined standard business visa procedures as an alternative. See: https://www.gov.pl/web/diplomacy/statement-on-suspending-mfa-participation-in-poland-business-harbour-programme

validation_notes: CORRECTION 2026-06-29 - Kosovo (XKX) notes updated: added biometric (ICAO-compliant) passport requirement, which is a condition per Regulation (EU) 2023/850. Source re-confirmed via EC Home Affairs announcement (home-affairs.ec.europa.eu/news/visa-free-travel-kosovo-citizens-eu-2024-01-03_en). Source URL updated to EC Home Affairs announcement page.

CORRECTION 2026-06-29 - Airport transit visa (A-type) country count corrected in notes: the previous text said '12 countries' but the list (Afghanistan, Armenia, Bangladesh, DRC, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Iran, Iraq, Nigeria, Pakistan, Somalia, Sri Lanka) is 13 nationalities. Re-confirmed on official gov.pl/usa-en/a-type-airport-transit-visa page (re-fetched 2026-06-29).

VERIFIED 2026-06-29 - Vanuatu: confirmed permanently removed from Annex II by EU Regulation 2025/11 (in force 3 February 2025). The UAE gov.pl embassy page still lists Vanuatu (outdated); the EU regulation takes precedence. Vanuatu correctly absent from visa_free array. Source: eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2025/11/oj/eng

VERIFIED 2026-06-29 - visa_free list cross-checked against gov.pl/canada-en/visa-free-countries and gov.pl/uae/list-of-countries-whose-citizens-can-travel-to-poland-without-visas. Both pages accessible. List consistent with current record except Vanuatu (still listed on UAE page, but EU Reg 2025/11 governs) and Kosovo (not on embassy pages, but EU Reg 2023/850 governs).

VERIFIED 2026-06-29 - Georgia ordinary-passport holders remain visa-free. EC Home Affairs confirmed suspension of visa-free for Georgian diplomatic/service/official passports only (6 March 2026 to 6 March 2027). The Georgia (GEO) visa_free entry correctly covers ordinary passports only.

VERIFIED 2026-06-29 - No e-visa or ETA system exists for Poland. ETIAS not yet operational. No CBI program. RBI and EU Blue Card entries not re-verified this run (mos.cudzoziemcy.gov.pl returned ECONNREFUSED); kept as-is per preservation rule.

CORRECTION 2026-06-27 - Kosovo (XKX) ADDED to visa_free list: Kosovo is listed in Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806 (consolidated text as of 03.02.2025). The footnote states the exemption applies from the date ETIAS starts operations or 1 January 2024, whichever is earlier. Since 1 January 2024 has passed, the exemption is now in force. Kosovo nationals can enter Schengen (including Poland) visa-free for up to 90 days in any 180-day period. Source: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX:02018R1806-20250203

CORRECTION 2026-06-27 - Airport transit visa notes corrected: the previous notes text erroneously listed Cuba and Egypt as A-type airport transit visa required nationalities. The official Polish government page gov.pl/web/usa-en/a-type-airport-transit-visa lists exactly 12 countries: Afghanistan, Armenia (from 1 August 2022), Bangladesh, Democratic Republic of Congo, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Iran, Iraq, Nigeria, Pakistan, Somalia, Sri Lanka. Cuba and Egypt are NOT on this official list. Notes text updated accordingly.

VERIFIED 2026-06-27 - Vanuatu confirmed REMOVED: Vanuatu was removed from Annex II (visa-free) by EU Regulation 2025/11 of 19 December 2024 (entering into force 3 February 2025), preceded by temporary suspension under delegated regulation from 4 August 2024. The gov.pl/canada-en, gov.pl/uae, and gov.pl/georgia embassy pages still show Vanuatu in their lists (not updated), but the EU regulation is the binding instrument. Correctly absent from visa_free array.

VERIFIED 2026-06-27 - Nauru: Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806 lists Nauru with a footnote that the exemption applies 'from the date of entry into force of an agreement on visa exemption to be concluded with the European Union.' No such agreement has entered into force. Nauru is NOT currently visa-free for Schengen. Correctly absent from visa_free.

VERIFIED 2026-06-27 - Three official Polish gov.pl embassy pages re-fetched (Canada, UAE, Georgia). All three still list Vanuatu and St. Pierre and Miquelon - both remain in error on those pages and have been correctly excluded from the visa_free array in this record.
VERIFIED 2026-06-27 - Conditional access entries (Schengen D-visa and Schengen residence permit holders) confirmed accurate per re-fetch of gov.pl/mswia-en.

CORRECTION - Vanuatu removed from visa_free list (prior run): Vanuatu was moved from Annex II (visa-free) to Annex I (visa-required) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1806 by EU Regulation 2025/11 adopted 12/19 December 2024, entering into force 3 February 2025. Source: https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2024/12/12/vanuatu-council-ends-visa-exemption/
CORRECTION - Saint Pierre and Miquelon removed from visa_free list (prior run): Not a sovereign nationality; French citizens use EU freedom of movement.

CORRECTION - Poland Business Harbour suspension source updated (prior run): suspended 26 January 2024. Source: https://www.gov.pl/web/diplomacy/statement-on-suspending-mfa-participation-in-poland-business-harbour-programme
VERIFIED - Sao Tome and Principe (STP) confirmed as visa-free on official gov.pl/georgia and gov.pl/canada embassy pages.
VERIFIED - Solomon Islands (SLB) confirmed as visa-free on official gov.pl/georgia and gov.pl/canada embassy pages; EU-Solomon Islands visa waiver agreement signed 7 October 2016.
VERIFIED - Dominican Republic (DOM) is NOT listed on any official Polish government page (gov.pl/canada, gov.pl/uae, gov.pl/georgia all checked). Not visa-free.

ADDED - ETIAS note: EU ETIAS system expected to launch mid-to-late 2026; once operational, visa-exempt non-EU nationals will need an ETIAS authorisation before entering Poland/Schengen. Not yet in force as of June 2026. Source: https://travel-europe.europa.eu/en/etias
VERIFIED - No e-visa or visa-on-arrival system exists for Poland; confirmed absent on official sources.
VERIFIED - CBI: no program exists; confirmed on official MoIA page.
VERIFIED - RBI business activity permits and EU Blue Card entries confirmed active on mos.cudzoziemcy.gov.pl.
NOTE - Kiribati: EU-Kiribati visa waiver agreement signed 24 June 2016 for completeness.
gaps: The official master visa-free list at msz.gov.pl/en/travel_to_poland/entering_poland/visa_free/visa_free_countries redirects to gov.pl/diplomacy without listing countries. List compiled from multiple official embassy-specific gov.pl subpages (UAE, Canada, Georgia missions) and EU Regulation 2018/1806 consolidated text.
Max stay days for EU/EEA citizens not specified (freedom of movement applies with no fixed cap for short stays).
Dominican Republic: confirmed absent from all official Polish government sources checked. No visa-free agreement with the EU is currently in force.

Vanuatu: some gov.pl embassy pages (Canada, Georgia, UAE) have not been updated and still list Vanuatu as visa-free despite EU Regulation 2025/11 removing it from Annex II as of 3 February 2025. The EU regulation is the binding instrument.

Nauru: listed in Annex II of EU Regulation 2018/1806 but with a footnote making the exemption conditional on a future visa waiver agreement with the EU entering into force. No such agreement currently in force. Not recorded as visa-free.
No official e-visa or ETA scheme currently exists for Poland; ETIAS not yet operational (expected mid-to-late 2026).
No official golden visa or CBI program; confirmed absent on official MoIA citizenship page.
Poland Business Harbour suspended since 26 January 2024; no active startup visa alternative announced yet on official sources.
Minimum investment threshold for business activity residence permit not stated numerically in official MOS portal; only income/revenue requirements specified.
No official Polish government source found confirming visa-free entry for holders of US, Canada, UK, Japan, or Australia residence permits for general visits. Not recorded.
No official Polish government source confirms OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) cardholder entry privileges for Poland. Not recorded.

Bilateral diplomatic/service-passport-only visa waiver agreements (e.g. reported Poland-Saudi Arabia January 2026): not confirmed on any official gov.pl or msz.gov.pl domain. Not recorded under official-source-first rule.

Airport transit visa (A-type) country list: Cuba and Egypt were previously included in notes in error; they are not on the official gov.pl/usa-en A-type list. The definitive 13-nationality list is per gov.pl/web/usa-en/a-type-airport-transit-visa (re-fetched 2026-06-27, re-confirmed 2026-06-29).

Georgia: the EU Commission suspended visa-free travel for holders of Georgian diplomatic, service, and official passports effective 6 March 2026 to 6 March 2027. This affects only those passport types; ordinary Georgian passport holders remain visa-free for Schengen. The current record correctly covers only ordinary passports and the Georgia entry remains valid.

visa_types.name: Schengen Visa (C-Type) – Tourist/Visit
visa_types.purpose: Short-stay visa for tourism, leisure, and social visits to Poland and the Schengen Area. Permits stays of up to 90 days within any 180-day period.
visa_types.notes: Standard Schengen C-type visa. Fee is EUR 90 for adults (approx. USD 98), EUR 45 for children 6–12 years, free for under 6. Single or multiple-entry options available. Applications must be submitted in person at a Polish consulate via the e-Konsulat booking system no earlier than 6 months and no later than 15 days before travel. Standard processing 15 calendar days; may extend to 30–45 days in exceptional cases.

visa_types.name: Schengen Visa (C-Type) – Business
visa_types.purpose: Short-stay visa for business purposes including commercial meetings, conferences, and negotiations with Polish partners. Permits stays up to 90 days within any 180-day period.
visa_types.notes: Subcategory of C-type Schengen visa for business purposes. Same fee structure and processing as tourist C-type (EUR 90 adults). Multiple-entry versions commonly issued for frequent business travelers.

visa_types.name: Schengen Visa (C-Type) – Transit
visa_types.purpose: Short-stay visa for travelers passing through Polish or Schengen territory en route to a third country. Permits transit through the Schengen Area.
visa_types.notes: C-type transit subcategory for passage through Schengen territory. Standard Schengen visa fees and processing apply. Not to be confused with the A-type airport transit visa.

visa_types.name: Airport Transit Visa (A-Type)
visa_types.purpose: Visa for transit through the international transit area of a Polish (Schengen) airport without entering the Schengen Area. Required for nationals of certain countries even when not leaving the transit zone.
visa_types.notes: Required only for nationals of (13 nationalities): Afghanistan, Armenia (from 1 August 2022), Bangladesh, Democratic Republic of Congo, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Ghana, Iran, Iraq, Nigeria, Pakistan, Somalia, Sri Lanka. Exemptions exist for diplomatic passport holders, EU family members, holders of valid C/D-type Schengen visas, and holders of valid residence permits or visas from Canada, Japan, San Marino, the US, or EEA countries. Fee USD 106 (US consulate); appeal/reconsideration fee USD 84. Processing 15 calendar days; may extend to 30–60 days. Does not authorise entry into the Schengen Area.

visa_types.name: National Visa (D-Type) – Study
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay national visa for foreign nationals pursuing full-time education at Polish higher education institutions, including degree programs, PhD studies, exchange programs (Erasmus, NAWA), and language courses.
visa_types.notes: Subcategories include: Study I year (beginning students), Study II year and above, and Study – Others (PhD, exchange, Erasmus, NAWA). Fee is EUR 100 in most jurisdictions (USD ~109); USD 235 at US consulates; GBP 175 in UK; free for Ukrainian nationals. Valid up to 1 year; holders may also stay in other Schengen states up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Processing 15 calendar days; up to 30 days for complex cases. Applications via e-Konsulat system in person at Polish consulate.

visa_types.name: National Visa (D-Type) – Work
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay national visa for foreign nationals taking up employment in Poland, requiring a valid work permit or declaration of intent to entrust work from a Polish employer.
visa_types.notes: Requires prior work permit (Zezwolenie na pracę) or employer declaration (oświadczenie o powierzeniu wykonywania pracy). Fee USD 235 at US consulate; EUR 100 (~USD 109) at most European consulates. Valid for up to 1 year. Processing 15 working days standard; expedited in 3 working days for urgent cases. Does not include EU Blue Card (fast-track highly-qualified worker stream, processed separately via MOS portal).

visa_types.name: National Visa (D-Type) – Family Member of Polish National
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay visa for foreign family members of Polish citizens who wish to join or visit their Polish relative in Poland for a period exceeding 90 days.
visa_types.notes: Fee GBP 175 at UK consulate; varies by consulate elsewhere. Fee may be waived in certain circumstances. Applicants must demonstrate family relationship with a Polish citizen. Valid up to 1 year. For longer stays, a temporary residence permit application may be required in Poland.

visa_types.name: National Visa (D-Type) – Pole's Card (Karta Polaka)
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay visa issued to holders of the Pole's Card (Karta Polaka), a document confirming Polish heritage or membership in the Polish nation for persons from former Soviet Union countries.
visa_types.notes: Available to Karta Polaka holders from countries of the former Soviet Union (Armenia, Azerbaijan, Belarus, Estonia, Georgia, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Latvia, Lithuania, Moldova, Russia, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Ukraine, Uzbekistan). Fee may be reduced or waived for Karta Polaka holders. Provides certain privileges including reduced visa fees and right to work without a separate work permit.

visa_types.name: National Visa (D-Type) – Medical Professional
visa_types.purpose: Long-stay national visa for foreign-qualified doctors, dentists, nurses, obstetricians, and paramedics seeking to practice their profession in Poland following recognition of their qualifications by the Minister of Health.
visa_types.notes: Requires prior approval from the Minister of Health for recognition of non-EU qualifications. Expedited processing of approximately 4 working days. Eligible professions: doctor, dentist, nurse, obstetrician, paramedic. Applicants from Ukraine, Belarus, Russia, Moldova, Georgia, and Armenia may submit applications by post; others must appear in person. Full consular fee schedule applies (same D-type fee structure).

visa_types.name: National Visa (D-Type) – NATO/SOFA
visa_types.purpose: National visa issued to NATO military and civilian personnel and their dependants stationed in or transiting through Poland under the NATO Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA).
visa_types.notes: Issued to NATO/SOFA personnel and dependants. Fee exemptions may apply under SOFA provisions. Applicants must present NATO orders or equivalent official documentation.

visa_types.name: Schengen Visa (C-Type) / National Visa (D-Type) – Diplomatic/Service
visa_types.purpose: Visas issued to holders of diplomatic or service passports for official missions, including diplomatic representatives, consular officers, and accredited international organization staff.
visa_types.notes: Fee exempt under the Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations. Diplomatic passport holders from most countries are also exempt from standard Schengen visa requirements. Issued via the Protocol Department of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs for accredited diplomats; consulates for service passport holders. Bilateral diplomatic visa waiver agreements may apply.

visa_types.name: National Visa (D-Type) – Other (General/Miscellaneous)
visa_types.purpose: Catch-all category of the Polish national D-type visa covering purposes not listed separately, such as cultural activities, sports competitions, volunteering, internships, and other justified long-stay purposes.
visa_types.notes: Used for purposes such as cultural, sports, scientific, religious, and humanitarian activities. Same fee structure and processing times as other D-type categories. Applicants must provide documents proving their specific purpose of stay.
